reasoning: Conflict event counts are typically right-skewed (positively skewed), meaning they feature occasional large spikes that pull the mean higher than the median. Because of this distribution, any random 30-day period naturally has a less than 50% chance of clearing the 360-day average. Furthermore, the 360-day baseline period preceding April 13, 2025, heavily features the 2024 Indian general elections (April 19 - June 1), which usually act as a massive catalyst for political violence, mob clashes, and targeted civilian attacks (particularly in highly contested states like West Bengal). Because the 69.500 reference average is inflated by this nationwide event, non-election months in the near term (April-July 2025) are heavily expected to fall short of this average. As the forecast horizon extends to October 2025, the probability increases and approaches 0.5 due to inherently higher uncertainty and the approaching Bihar state assembly elections (October/November 2025), which historically bring elevated risks of localized and political violence that could push the 30-day tally back above the threshold.
